what_manipulated: age group (younger vs. older adults)
what_measured: number of episodic (internal) and contextual (external) details generated in autobiographical narratives
experiment: Units of different ages were asked to generate past and future autobiographical events. Transcriptions were segmented into details and classified as internal (episodic) or external. The number of details was compared between age groups and across past/future event types. Correlations between detail counts and relational memory abilities were also examined.

what_manipulated: target viewpoint (prototypical vs. nonprototypical views) and target definition (name-based vs. action-based)
what_measured: fixation probability and fixation duration during visual search
experiment: Units performed visual search for targets defined either by their names or by the action typically performed on them. Targets appeared in prototypical or nonprototypical views. Eye movements were recorded. First-fixation probability and fixation duration were analyzed by target view and definition type, stratified by visual field location.

what_manipulated: exposure to text promoting a belief in determinism vs. neutral text
what_measured: cheating behavior and belief in free will
experiment: Units were assigned to read either deterministic or neutral messages. In Experiment 1, cheating was measured via a task where a flawed computer program could reveal answers; mediation analysis tested whether reduced free-will belief explained increased cheating. In Experiment 2, cheating was measured via self-reported payment for cognitive performance. Tests included comparisons of cheating rates and mediation analysis.

what_manipulated: stimulus onset asynchrony (SOA) between two targets in rapid serial visual presentation
what_measured: detection accuracy and distractor intrusions at different temporal intervals
experiment: Units viewed rapid sequences of items and reported two targets (T1 and T2) presented at varying SOAs (roughly 200-500 ms). Distractor intrusions were analyzed as a function of SOA to estimate temporal selection suppression, latency, and precision. Results were described as dissociated time-course effects.

what_manipulated: presence or absence of extremal edge (viewpoint-specific self-occlusion cue) on a surface border; cue type (shading vs. texture gradient)
what_measured: depth perception and figure-ground organization judgments
experiment: Units viewed images containing surfaces with and without extremal edges along shared borders. In separate experiments, cues were provided via shading or texture gradients. Units judged relative depth and figure-ground assignment. Comparisons tested whether extremal-edge surfaces were perceived as closer and as figure.

what_manipulated: number of tones in a chord and interstimulus interval (ISI) duration
what_measured: change-detection accuracy for frequency shifts
experiment: Units made same/different judgments on successive chords and rated tone-frequency comparisons. The change was always a frequency shift in one tone. Complexity (number of tones) and ISI (0 ms vs. 2 s) were varied. Performance was compared across complexity and ISI conditions.

what_manipulated: object words denoting spatial locations (head vs. foot, high vs. low objects)
what_measured: target-identification accuracy and speed for visual stimuli
experiment: Units viewed object words and subsequently identified visual targets presented at various vertical positions. The prediction was that words encoding typical object locations would orient attention and activate spatial simulations, impairing target identification when targets appeared at the word's typical location. Identification accuracy was compared across word-target congruence conditions.

what_manipulated: external motivation to respond without prejudice (low vs. high) and face race and expression (White/Black, neutral/happy) and presentation duration (30 ms vs. 450 ms)
what_measured: attentional bias on a dot-probe task
experiment: Units with low and high external motivation were presented with face pairs (White and Black, neutral or happy) at two durations. A probe followed, and reaction times indicated attentional bias. Attentional patterns were compared across motivation level, race, expression, and duration conditions.

what_manipulated: commission of errors vs. correct responses during a task
what_measured: error-related negativity (ERN) amplitude and defensive startle response magnitude
experiment: Units performed a task inducing errors. Event-related potentials (ERP) were recorded to measure ERN. Startle magnitude was measured following errors and correct responses. Regression analysis tested whether ERN amplitude predicted individual differences in startle potentiation after errors.

what_manipulated: induced loneliness (chronically lonely vs. experimentally primed) and exposure condition (observational vs. experimental for Studies 2-3)
what_measured: anthropomorphism and belief in humanlike nonhuman agents
experiment: Units were assessed for chronic loneliness (Study 1) or induced to feel lonely (Studies 2-3). Anthropomorphic judgments and belief in humanlike agents were measured. Three studies examined whether loneliness increased attributions of humanlike qualities. Study 3 tested whether the effect was specific to negative affect or a general negative-state effect.

what_manipulated: precision of numerical anchor (precise vs. rounded)
what_measured: magnitude of adjustment away from anchor in numerical judgments
experiment: Units made numerical estimates after exposure to either precise or rounded anchors. Five studies varied anchor precision and measurement domains. Adjustment magnitude was compared between anchor-precision conditions. Subjective scale resolution was inferred from patterns of adjustment.

what_manipulated: male relationship status (single vs. attached) and female menstrual-cycle phase (low vs. high fertility)
what_measured: attractiveness ratings of males
experiment: Partnered women (N=208) rated attractiveness of men described as single or attached. Phase of menstrual cycle was assessed. Attractiveness ratings were compared across male-status and cycle-phase conditions, stratified by male masculinity (genetic-quality cue).

what_manipulated: evaluative goal (positive, negative, or overall evaluation of names) during fMRI
what_measured: amygdala activation in response to positive and negative stimuli
experiment: Units performed fMRI while evaluating famous names along different goal dimensions. Amygdala activation was measured in response to positive and negative names under each goal condition. Within-subject contrasts tested whether activation patterns varied by evaluative goal and stimulus valence.

what_manipulated: cognitive fluency (fluent vs. disfluent stimulus processing) induced via visual perceptual, conceptual priming, or linguistic manipulations
what_measured: level of mental construal (concrete vs. abstract) and perceived psychological distance
experiment: Three studies manipulated fluency via different mechanisms: visual perceptual fluency, conceptual priming, and linguistic fluency. Units processed stimuli and made construal judgments or distance estimates. Construal level and distance judgments were compared between fluency conditions.

what_manipulated: encoding task (survival relevance rating vs. imagery rating, pleasantness rating, self-reference rating, word generation, intentional learning, or scenario relevance rating)
what_measured: retention of words on a later memory test
experiment: Units encoded words under different task conditions. Retention was later tested. Memory performance was compared across encoding conditions, with survival processing pitted against multiple control conditions known to produce strong retention.

what_manipulated: speaker visibility (speaker exits/returns to room) and linguistic frame (statement 'there's...' vs. question 'where's...') and object visibility configuration
what_measured: selection of target object on word-learning trials based on speaker's gaze
experiment: Two experiments manipulated what the speaker could see and the linguistic framing of the novel word. Units (ages 2-4) heard a novel word with varied speaker context. Target selection was compared across visibility and linguistic conditions, and above-chance performance was tested.

what_manipulated: language (French vs. English) and naming task (color naming vs. object naming) and phonological similarity between color and object names
what_measured: phonological congruency effect on naming latency
experiment: Units from French and English-speaking populations named colored objects. Phonological similarity between color and object names was manipulated. Naming latencies were compared across language, task, and phonological-similarity conditions. Word-order constraints were inferred from cross-linguistic contrasts.

what_manipulated: behavioral information about group members (positive vs. negative behaviors) and time delay (immediate vs. days later) and evaluation type (implicit vs. explicit)
what_measured: attitude toward new group members (generalization from original member)
experiment: Units learned positive and negative behaviors performed by individuals from two groups. Implicit and explicit evaluations of new group members were assessed immediately and days later. Generalization was compared across time, evaluation type, and group-membership link.

what_manipulated: glucose ingestion (sugar vs. sugar substitute) and prior self-control task (depleted vs. not depleted)
what_measured: susceptibility to the attraction effect (decoy bias) in decision making
experiment: Units consumed either sugar-sweetened or sugar-free lemonade. Before the decision task, some units performed a self-control task. Units then made choices between options where a decoy option was present. Attraction-effect magnitude was compared across glucose and depletion conditions.

what_manipulated: priming of nonconscious achievement goals vs. control priming during exposure to novel environments
what_measured: implicit learning of environmental structure
experiment: Two experiments using markedly different implicit-learning paradigms primed nonconscious achievement goals or control conditions. Units were exposed to completely novel environments. Learning of environmental structure was assessed. Comparisons tested whether goal priming facilitated implicit learning.
